planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile, moist to moderately dry soil with a sturdy trellis or support in zone 9–11; Port St. John's Creeper (Zimbabwe Creeper, Pink Trumpet Vine) is a spectacular, vigorous ornamental climbing vine from South Africa and Zimbabwe — producing large, showy, trumpet-shaped flowers in rosy-pink to soft lilac-pink with darker pink veining in loose terminal clusters throughout summer and fall — one of the most beautiful of all tropical climbing vines.

directions: Plant Podranea ricasoliana in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil with a very sturdy trellis, pergola, or wall to support its vigorous growth; train the initial stems to the support and allow to mature for 2–3 years before expecting heavy bloom; cut back in late winter to encourage compact, manageable growth and maximize flowering. Port St. John's Creeper is named for the coastal town in the Eastern Cape of South Africa near the Pondoland coast, where it scrambles over scrub and along forest margins. The large, broadly funnel-shaped flowers in soft rosy-pink to lilac-pink are produced in loose clusters at the branch tips from late spring through fall; individual flowers are 2–3 inches across, giving the vine a generous, lush appearance when in full bloom. It is similar to the more commonly grown Bignonia capreolata and related trumpet vines but with distinctly softer, more pastel coloring that blends beautifully with other plants. In zone 9, it dies back to the root crown after hard frost and re-emerges in spring; in zone 10–11 it grows as a large, semi-evergreen perennial vine reaching 15–20 feet or more. Butterflies visit the flowers and the vine is generally pest- and disease-resistant in suitable climates. A superb choice for pergolas, fences, walls, and large trellises in Gulf Coast subtropical gardens.
sources: South African National Biodiversity Institute (SANBI) — Podranea ricasoliana
University of Florida IFAS Extension — flowering vines for South Florida

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Costa Rican Guava (Cas Guava, Cas, Guayaba Agria) is a small to medium fruit tree native to Costa Rica, Panama, and adjacent Central America — highly important in Costa Rican national cuisine and culture — bearing abundant small, tart, white-fleshed fruits with a refreshing, lemon-bright guava flavor that is more acidic than common guava, prized for fresh juice, agua de cas, and in cooking.

directions: Plant Psidium friedrichsthalianum in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly during establishment and fruit development; tolerates some frost once established but best in zone 10–12; harvest fruits when they turn fully yellow and give slightly under gentle pressure. Costa Rican Guava ('cas' in local parlance — a word so fundamental it has no direct English translation outside of the botanical name) occupies a unique place in Costa Rican culture — the guava-sized, white-fleshed, intensely tart fruits are the basis of agua fresca de cas (the most popular fresh juice in the country), used in refresco mixtures, ice creams, jams, and as a souring agent in cooking. The flavor is bracingly tart and intensely guava-fragrant, with significantly more acidity than the common tropical guava (Psidium guajava) — more similar to a tart lemon guava in flavor profile. The tree is somewhat smaller and more cold-tolerant than the common guava, performing well in humid subtropical conditions. In zone 10–12 Gulf Coast gardens and Florida, it fruits reliably and heavily with minimal care. The fruit's high pectin content makes it excellent for jams and jellies without needing commercial pectin. For Costa Rican immigrants and lovers of Central American cuisine, this tree represents home in a way that the common guava cannot replicate.
sources: UCR (University of Costa Rica) — Psidium friedrichsthalianum agricultural study
CATIE (Centro Agronómico Tropical) — cas guava research
Morton, Julia F., 'Fruits of Warm Climates' (1987) — Psidium friedrichsthalianum

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in zone 9–12; Savanna Guava (Guisaro, Brazilian Guava, Cas Silvestre) is a sprawling, drought-resistant shrub or small tree from the tropical savannas (cerrado) of Brazil and adjacent South America — producing abundant small, round, yellow-green fruits with sweet-tart, white to cream flesh and a pronounced guava aroma — highly drought and cold tolerant compared to common guava.

directions: Plant Psidium guineense in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic, lean soil in full sun; once established it is highly drought-tolerant — one of the most drought-resistant of all Psidium species — and performs well in sandy, poor soils where common guava struggles; prune lightly after fruiting to maintain a manageable size. Savanna Guava is the wild guava of the Brazilian cerrado (tropical savanna) — a shrubby, fire-resistant, drought-adapted species that has co-evolved with the deep-rooted, seasonally dry ecosystems of central Brazil. The small (golf-ball-sized to slightly larger), yellow-green fruits are produced in abundance from a relatively young age; the flavor is sweet-tart with an assertive, complex guava aroma — often described as more intensely flavored than common guava — and the fruits are eaten fresh, used for juice, made into jellies, and fermented into guava wine. The plant's tolerance of poor, sandy, acidic soils and periods of summer drought make it an excellent choice for Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens where common guava might be marginal; it is hardy to about 25–27°F with protection. The white, four-petaled flowers are attractive and produced throughout the warm season; birds relish the ripe fruits. A productive, low-maintenance, wildlife-supporting fruit shrub for hot, dry garden conditions.
sources: Embrapa Cerrados (Brazil) — Psidium guineense cerrado fruits research
University of Florida IFAS — Psidium guineense minor tropical fruits

subcategory: Central American small citrus-flavored Garcinia fruit

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in zone 10–12; Lemon Drop Mangosteen (Imbe, Chaparrón) is a small- to medium-fruiting Garcinia from the humid lowland forests of Costa Rica, Panama, Guatemala, and Belize — producing clusters of small, round, bright-golden-yellow to orange fruits with sweet, intensely citrus-flavored, translucent-white pulp surrounding a single seed — a unique and highly esteemed rare fruit in Central American tropical gardens.

directions: Plant Garcinia intermedia in fertile, moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; provide consistent moisture throughout the year — Garcinia species do not tolerate prolonged drought; protect from cold below 40°F, especially in youth; dioecious species (both male and female plants needed for fruiting, or select self-fertile named varieties if available). Lemon Drop Mangosteen is one of the most exquisitely flavored of all Garcinia fruits — smaller than the Purple Mangosteen (Garcinia mangostana) but with a uniquely intense, sweet-tart, citrus-dominant flavor profile quite different from other Garcinias. The bright golden-yellow to orange, golf-ball-sized fruits contain translucent-white, juicy pulp segments of remarkable flavor — combining intense lemon, lime, and sweet tropical fruit notes in an almost sherbet-like profile. In Central America, the fruits are eaten fresh, used in drinks, and made into preserves; the yellow skin contains bitter compounds similar to other Garcinias and is not eaten. In zone 10–12 gardens and collections, Lemon Drop Mangosteen grows as a slow-growing, handsome ornamental tree with attractive, glossy, dark-green leaves; fruiting begins after 5–7 years from seed. An excellent greenhouse tree in cooler climates.
sources: CATIE — Garcinia intermedia tropical fruits database
Rare Fruit Council International — Garcinia intermedia
Morton, Julia F., 'Fruits of Warm Climates' (1987) — Garcinia species

subcategory: East Asian small-fruiting Garcinia ornamental tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–11; Hong Kong Garcinia is a small to medium ornamental and fruiting tree native to southern China, Hong Kong, and Vietnam — bearing small clusters of bright-orange to red fruits with sweet-sour, translucent flesh above attractive, glossy, oblong leaves — relatively cold-tolerant compared to tropical Garcinias and adaptable to humid subtropical conditions.

directions: Plant Garcinia oblongifolia in humus-rich, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to dappled sun; maintain consistent soil moisture and avoid prolonged drought; protect young plants from temperatures below 32°F; established trees tolerate light frost. Hong Kong Garcinia is one of the more cold-hardy Garcinia species — adapted to the subtropical climate of South China and Hong Kong where winters can be cool with occasional brief frost — making it more tractable in Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens than strictly tropical Garcinias. The ornamental value is high: the tree has a neat, dense, compact form with attractive, glossy, oblong, dark-green leaves and produces small clusters of bright orange-red fruits that are decorative on the tree. The fruits have a sweet-sour, juicy, translucent-white pulp somewhat resembling a small mangosteen in flavor — less rich than Purple Mangosteen but pleasant and refreshing. The tree is appreciated in Hong Kong and southern Chinese traditional culture both as an ornamental and for the edible fruits; it also has traditional medicinal uses in Chinese herbal medicine, particularly the dried rind of the fruit (similar to Garcinia cambogia). Relatively uncommon in Western cultivation but deserves wider planting in humid subtropical gardens as a hardy, attractive alternative to more tropical Garcinias.

subcategory: Southeast Asian ornamental small-fruit persimmon tree

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in zone 10–12; Yellow Persimmon (Madan, Ten-stamen Persimmon, Burmese Persimmon) is an attractive ornamental and fruiting tree from Indochina, Thailand, and the Malay Peninsula — bearing round to oval, small, bright-yellow fruits with very sweet, yellow flesh and no astringency, prized as a fresh-eating persimmon across Southeast Asia and grown as both an ornamental and edible tree in tropical gardens.

directions: Plant Diospyros decandra in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly throughout the year; protect from temperatures below 40°F; harvest fruits when they reach full yellow color and have a slight give to finger pressure — they should be non-astringent even when firm, unlike temperate persimmons. Yellow Persimmon is an underappreciated tropical fruit tree that deserves far wider cultivation than it currently receives outside Southeast Asia. Unlike the more widely known Asian persimmon (Diospyros kaki) which is astringent when underripe, Yellow Persimmon (madan in Thai, 'ten stamen' in English — referring to its ten stamens vs. 16 in D. kaki) produces small, round, bright-yellow fruits that are sweet and non-astringent even when firm, and have a honey-sweet, mildly vanilla-flavored flesh. The fruits are eaten fresh in Thailand and throughout Indochina as a dessert fruit, and the tree is planted as both an ornamental (handsome, glossy-leaved, with fragrant, cream-white flowers) and a fruit tree in village gardens. The tree grows as a medium-sized, evergreen shade tree in the tropics, producing fruits that ripen over an extended season from late summer through winter. An excellent rare fruit tree for zone 10–12 tropical and subtropical gardens seeking both ornamental beauty and unusual fresh fruit.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Diospyros decandra
Thai Forest Research Institute — Diospyros decandra
Rare Fruit Council International — Diospyros species minor fruits
